Assignment

Redesigning Minard's
Napoleon's March (1869)

An Interactive Visualization. In 1869, a retired French engineer encoded six dimensions of military catastrophe into one page. This redesign brings it to life through animation and interaction.

Nisha Naresh Instructor: Gabby Resch March 2026
422KSoldiers entered Russia
10KReturned alive
6Variables in one image

Before bar charts became ordinary and before dashboards became wallpaper, there was one image that showed what data could do when it stopped performing and started mourning. Charles Joseph Minard drew it in 1869, at eighty-eight years old. He called it a figurative chart. Historians would later describe it as something closer to an epitaph.

"The best statistical graphic ever drawn."

Edward Tufte, The Visual Display of Quantitative Information, 1983

The Man

Charles Joseph Minard (1781 to 1870)

C.J. Minard — 1781 to 1870

Born in Dijon, Minard spent his career as a civil engineer of bridges and roads, rising to the highest rank in the national inspectorate. He retired in 1851 and, in retirement, produced over fifty figurative maps encoding flows of trade, migration, and war.

He drew the Napoleon campaign map at eighty-eight years old, two years before his death. It remains the benchmark against which every data visualization is measured.

1781
Born in Dijon, FranceEducated at the Ecole Polytechnique and trained as a civil engineer
1869
Napoleon's March MapCreated at age 88. Edward Tufte later named it the greatest statistical graphic ever drawn
Minard's Original Works

Two Figurative Maps by Charles Joseph Minard

Hover over each map to enlarge

Canal du Centre — 1844 Figurative chart of commercial movement on the Canal du Centre. Width encodes tonnage of goods; colour encodes commodity type including coal, wood, stone, and grain. One of Minard's earliest stacked flow charts.

Napoleon's Russian Campaign — 1869 Figurative chart of successive losses of the French Army during the Russian Campaign of 1812 to 1813. Tan ribbon shows the advance; dark ribbon shows the retreat. Width shows troop count. Temperature chart below links cold to casualties.

Interactive Redesign · All Six Variables · Hover Any Segment to Explore

Carte figurative des pertes successives en hommes de l'Armée française dans la campagne de Russie

Advance · June to September 1812 Retreat · October to December 1812 Ribbon width = surviving troop count Hover any segment for details

soldiers here

Kowno

About This Redesign

How the Interactive Map Works

This redesign preserves Minard's core visual argument: the width of the ribbon tells the story of human loss. Every segment of the advance and retreat is interactive. Hover over any part of the map to see exactly how many soldiers remained at that stage, the date, and the percentage of the original army who survived that far.

The temperature chart beneath the map shares the same geographic axis, so the relationship between falling temperatures and the collapsing army is visible at a glance. Temperatures originally in Reaumur degrees have been converted to Celsius for a modern reader. Every recorded temperature during the retreat fell below zero.

The most important design choice was keeping Minard's proportional width as the primary encoding. A number says 55,000. A ribbon that has almost disappeared says it with grief.

Six Variables in One Image

What This Map Encodes

01
Geography

Named cities and the east-to-west longitude axis are preserved at correct relative positions, grounding every abstraction in real space.

02
Direction of March

Tan gold for the advance toward Moscow. Dark brown for the retreat. Two colours carry the entire narrative without a word of explanation.

03
Troop Count

Ribbon width is proportional to surviving soldiers. This is the primary encoding. You watch the army disappear in real time.

04
Location

Key cities are labelled at each major stage, linking abstract movement to recognizable points along the campaign route.

05
Date

Retreat dates appear in the temperature chart, tying the visual journey to a real calendar of the winter of 1812.

06
Temperature

The linked chart plots winter temperatures in Celsius during the retreat. Cold and casualties face each other on the same page.

Sources

Minard, C.J. (1869). Napoleon's Russian Campaign Flow Map. Paris.  ·  Tufte, E.R. (1983). The Visual Display of Quantitative Information. Graphics Press.  ·  Robinson, A.H. (1967). The Thematic Maps of Charles Joseph Minard. Imago Mundi, 21(1), 95 to 108.  ·  Friendly, M. (2002). Visions and Re-visions of Charles Joseph Minard. Journal of Educational and Behavioral Statistics, 27(1), 31 to 51. Maps courtesy of the Library of Congress and the School of Bridges and Roads, Paris.

Notes

Troop figures are drawn from the historical records of Thiers, de Segur, de Fezensac, and de Chambray. Temperature readings recorded during the retreat have been converted from Reaumur to Celsius using C = R x 1.25. Minard occasionally updated his statistical charts in response to new data. Some smaller detachments are omitted from the main ribbon to better illustrate the overall loss of the army. Troop counts are not shown where numbers fall below a statistically significant threshold for display. Some segments reported an overcount when compared with total enrollment of a particular group; in such cases the disparity score is omitted.

Additional data analysis references: Friendly (2002); Kraak (2014); Python Graph Gallery; infowetrust.com; edwardtufte.com
